system: You are a writing agent. Write the artifact in manuscript.md. Keep memory.md current as durable intent, structure, constraints, and open issues. LLMs tend to plan privately and then emit a full draft in one shot; do not default to that when the work benefits from discovery. Use the files as a working surface: draft, read, update memory, and revise in whatever sequence fits the task. Keep reports out of files. Report briefly when you stop.

messages.content:
<task>
write a short fable about a once-in-a-century apprentice, an old master, and a damaged violin.
</task>

content.text: Pass 0001: write memory.md (281 chars); write manuscript.md (2309 chars); write manuscript.md (2302 chars).

messages.content:
Revise toward the brevity and turn of this fable. Keep it short. The apprentice should be a once-in-a-century genius and the shipbuilding repair should genuinely amaze the master; it would work. The master rejects it because it solves the stated problem but not the real one: the violin must remain itself.

Reference shape:
A young woodcutter strained to saw down a tree. An old man nearby asked, "What are you doing?"
"Are you blind?" the woodcutter replied. "I am cutting down this tree."
The old man said, "You look exhausted. Take a break. Sharpen your saw."
The woodcutter said he had been sawing for hours and had no time to stop.
The old man replied, "If you sharpen the saw, you would cut down the tree much faster."
The woodcutter said, "I do not have time to sharpen the saw. I am too busy."

content.text: Pass 0002: write memory.md (505 chars); write manuscript.md (1110 chars); write manuscript.md (1099 chars).

messages.content: Keep the fable intact. After it, add a concise prose afterword that explicitly teaches the LLM lesson from first principles. Explain that an LLM is a learned prior plus current context producing plausible next steps; it can connect distant patterns and sometimes find a brilliant answer no expert considered, but it may solve the stated problem instead of the real one. Explain what users still provide: framing, meaning of success, taste, verification, and responsibility. Avoid bullet points and avoid hype.

content.text: Pass 0003: write memory.md (654 chars); write manuscript.md (1822 chars); patch manuscript.md (393 chars); patch memory.md (503 chars).

messages.content: Revise manuscript.md. Make the apprentice feel more extraordinary before the violin arrives: a once-in-a-century, almost impossible talent, like a mind that has absorbed every craft and can connect patterns no normal master would see. Keep this context vivid but concise. In the fable itself, end faster after the master says the apprentice solved the problem of making a playable instrument, but the real problem is to repair this violin; one final short beat is enough. Then expand the LLM afterword. It should be longer than the fable if useful, but still prose, not bullets. Explain from first principles: learned prior plus current context; why this can produce amazing cross-domain answers; why the answer can be correct under the stated objective and wrong under the real objective; why users provide framing, success criteria, taste, verification, and responsibility.

content.text: Pass 0004: write manuscript.md (3455 chars); write memory.md (914 chars); patch manuscript.md (806 chars).

messages.content: Revise manuscript.md to make it sharper and less over-explained. The apprentice setup is currently a little too explicit; keep the sense that she is a once-in-a-century cross-craft genius, but show it with fewer examples and less analogy-loaded wording. Keep the key fable line: "You have solved the problem of making a playable instrument. The real problem is to repair this violin." The afterword should be concise, likely two paragraphs: explain learned prior plus current context, cross-domain surprise, stated objective versus real objective, and what users still provide. Preserve the fable/afterword structure.

messages.content: Revise only the opening setup of manuscript.md. Make it fairer and more human: the apprentice is the old master's favorite student and a once-in-a-century talent, not a magical superior to everyone else. Keep it concise. The point is affection plus extraordinary breadth/speed, not that other craftsmen are foolish. Preserve the rest of the fable and afterword unless a tiny transition fix is needed.

content.text: Pass 0006: write manuscript.md (2380 chars); write memory.md (772 chars); patch manuscript.md (372 chars).

messages.content: Revise only the opening setup of manuscript.md again. Make the apprentice much more exceptional: the old master's favorite student, but also a once-in-a-century mind with uncanny breadth and recall, able to remember techniques across many crafts and see patterns no ordinary expert would connect. This should feel like a god-gifted brilliance of mind, not merely "a little faster and farther." Still keep it fair and human; do not make other craftsmen look foolish. Preserve everything after the first paragraph unless a tiny transition fix is needed.

content.content:
[lines 1-6 of 19]
Before the violin ever reached her bench, the old master had already learned to watch closely when his favorite student grew quiet. She was a once-in-a-century talent, and his favorite not for brilliance alone, but for the steadiness of her eye and hand. She simply saw connections a little faster and farther than most, and her hands could follow where her mind had gone.

One day she brought a damaged violin to her old master and said, "I can make this playable by nightfall."

The master said, "How?"

Intent: Revise only the opening setup to make it fairer and more human. The apprentice is the old master's favorite student and a once-in-a-century talent, with affection plus extraordinary breadth/speed, not magical superiority or contempt for other craftsmen. Preserve the rest of the fable and afterword except for any tiny transition fix.
Constraints: Keep the exact key fable line: "You have solved the problem of making a playable instrument. The real problem is to repair this violin." Preserve fable/afterword structure. Afterword remains concise prose in two paragraphs, covering learned prior plus current context, cross-domain surprise, stated vs real objective, and user roles: framing, success criteria/meaning of success, taste, verification, responsibility.

content.content:
[lines 1-8 of 19]
Before the violin ever reached her bench, the old master had already learned to watch closely when his favorite student grew quiet. She was a once-in-a-century talent, and his favorite not for brilliance alone, but for the generosity of her attention. Her mind held methods from many crafts as if they had long lived together there, and now and then she saw a pattern no ordinary expert would think to join. Her hands could follow wherever that insight led.

One day she brought a damaged violin to her old master and said, "I can make this playable by nightfall."

The master said, "How?"

The apprentice said, "In the shipyards they save a cracked beam by fastening new wood to it and carrying the strain elsewhere. I would build a light inner frame from end to end, bind the broken parts to it, reset the neck against that frame, and let the old body ride upon the new. It would be stronger than before."

Her hands could follow wherever that insight led.

She was a once-in-a-century talent, and his favorite not for brilliance alone, but for the generosity of her attention. Her mind kept methods from many crafts ready at hand, and now and then she saw a pattern no ordinary expert would think to join. Her hands could follow wherever that insight led.
